text: We begin by placing a port in the 8th intercostal space in the posterior axillary line.
We placed our utility port anteriorly in the 5th intercostal space and used an extra small wound protector as well.
You can see here the large hiatal hernia as well as the esophagus to the left of the aorta.
We begin by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament from its attachments on the hernia sac.
We then follow this dissection up to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
You can see here the inferior pulmonary vein as well as some associated lymph nodes.
Here you can see a clear view of the esophagus and its posterior attachments to the aorta.
We begin by opening the posterior
between the esophagus and the aorta with cautery.
We open this window circumferentially around the esophagus using a harmonic as well as blunt dissection.
Our goal at this step of the operation is to create enough space around the esophagus to place a Penrose drain for retraction.
We elect to staple our Penrose drain just for ease of use to keep the tails out of the way.
Using the drain to retract the esophagus, we extend our dissection superiorly towards the level of the arch.
Here you'll see the recurrent laryngeal nerve begin to come into view as well as the pulmonary artery.
Here is a great view of that anatomy.
We then extend our dissection anteriorly and posteriorly around the esophagus, cranially towards the level of the arch.
The goal here is to create as much mobilization under the arch as possible in order to avoid having to dissect in this space from a cervical incision.
This portion of the operation does take quite a bit of time and some patience.
Our ultimate goal is for a tension-free cervical anastomosis.
We then turn our attention inferiorly.
Because this is a reoperative field, there is quite a bit of adhesive tissue.
We begin by taking down the adhesions between the hernia sac and the pericardium, taking care to avoid the phrenic neurovascular bundle, which you can see just in view.
We also notice quite a bit of adhesive tissue between the esophagus and the aorta, as well as the diaphragm.
We dissect the inferior aspect of the hernia sac off the diaphragm circumferentially, which does take quite a bit of time.
And we do return to this dissection several times throughout this case.
And you'll notice there is
mutations as well, posteriorly between the hernia sac and the aorta, which are also carefully taken down using energy in order to avoid any bleeding from posterior perforators to the esophagus.
As you can see here, there are some attachments between the hernia sac and the contents of the right hemithorax.
So we do see the right lung begin to come into view.
So we take some time freeing the hernia sac from the right pleura as well as the right lung.

The extensive adhesions between the esophagus and the contents of the right hemothorax were somewhat unexpected at the beginning of this case, but were taken down carefully, allowing ourselves to mobilize the esophagus as much as possible.

After very extensive dissection, you'll notice that we finally have a freely mobile esophagus, free from its attachments to the contents of the right hemithorax, including the right lung and the right pleura, as well as contents of the left hemithorax, including the pericardium, the diaphragm, as well as the aorta itself.
We also have excellent mobilization extending cranially under the level of the arch to allow for a tension-free cervical anastomosis.

You can also see a great view of the anatomy, including the pulmonary artery, the trachea, as well as the recurrent laryngeal nerve, which has remained uninjured, and the pericardium with the phrenic neurovascular bundle intact as well.
At this point, we were satisfied with our mobilization and we proceeded with the abdominal and cervical portions of this case.
Those proceeded without issue and the patient did very well.
